perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and constituted and enforced in accordance with the internal laws of the State of North Carolina , and shall be binding upon the parties hereto in the United States and worldwide. Failure to enforce any provision of this Agreement shall not constitute a waiver of any other term hereof. The Parties agree to submit to the jurisdiction of any state or federal court sitting in North Carolina . Each party irrevocably waives any right that it may have to a trial by jur y in connection with any dispute arising out of or in connection with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and constituted and enforced in accordance with the internal laws of the State of North Carolina , and shall be binding upon the parties hereto in the United States and worldwide. Failure to enforce any provision of this Agreement shall not constitute a waiver of any other term hereof. The Parties agree to submit to the jurisdiction of any state or federal court sitting in North Carolina .
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause 'Each party irrevocably waives any right that it may have to a trial by jury in connection with any dispute arising out of or in connection with this Agreement' may be unenforceable under North Carolina law and federal law if not knowingly and voluntarily made. This omission removes an important protection against an unknowing waiver of rights.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 7th Amendment to the US Constitution and North Carolina law regarding jury trial waivers
perturbation.law_explanation: The 7th Amendment guarantees the right to a jury trial in civil cases. North Carolina law requires waivers to be knowing, intelligent, and voluntary. Removing this clause without other protections may violate these requirements if a party is unaware they are giving up this right.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Seventh Amendment | U.S. Constitution | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Seventh Amendment The Seventh Amendment continues a practice from English common law of distinguishing civil claims which must be tried before a jury (absent waiver by the parties) from claims and issues that may be heard by a judge alone. It only governs federal civil courts and has no application to civil courts set up by the states when those courts are hearing only disputes of state law . Learn more... Primary tabs Amendment VII In suits at common law, where the value in controversy shall exceed twenty dollars, the right of trial by jury shall be preserved, and no fact tried by a jury, shall be otherwise reexamined in any court of the United States, than according to the rules of the common law. Wex Resources Criminal Law Criminal Procedure Jury Other Resources US Code: Title 18 Federal Rules of Criminal Procedure ‹ Sixth Amendment up Eighth Amendment › U.S. Constitution Toolbox Explanation of the Constitution - from the Congressional Research Service
perturbation.justification: The original text included a waiver of the right to a jury trial, while the changed text omits this waiver. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both highlight that such waivers must be knowing and voluntary. The scraped law snippet from the Seventh Amendment states that the right to trial by jury shall be preserved. By removing the explicit waiver, the changed text creates a situation where a party might unknowingly waive this right, potentially contradicting the requirement for a knowing and voluntary waiver as implicitly protected by the Seventh Amendment, and explained in the prompt.

perturbation.original_text: Prior to providing any Confidential Information to employees or consultants, the receiving Party shall notify such persons that such information is Confidential Information and must be kept in confidence as set forth in this Agreement. The receiving Party shall be responsible for any breach of this agreement by such P arty's employees and consultants.
perturbation.changed_text: Prior to providing any Confidential Information to employees or consultants, the receiving Party shall notify such persons that such information is Confidential Information and must be kept in confidence as set forth in this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause stating "The receiving Party shall be responsible for any breach of this agreement by such Party's employees and consultants", the agreement now fails to explicitly hold the receiving party liable for breaches caused by their employees or consultants, which removes a legally important responsibility.
perturbation.contradicted_law: North Carolina General Statute § 75-1.1 regarding unfair trade practices.
perturbation.law_explanation: NCGS § 75-1.1 prohibits unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in commerce. If a company fails to adequately protect confidential information (as shown by not taking responsibility for employee/consultant breaches), and that failure results in competitive harm, it could be seen as an unfair trade practice. Removing the explicit responsibility weakens the protection and could contradict this statute.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: ï»¿ G.S. 75-1.1 § 75-1.1. Methods of competition, acts and practices regulated; legislative policy. (a) Unfair methods of competition in or affecting commerce, and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in or affecting commerce, are declared unlawful. (b) For purposes of this section, "commerce" includes all business activities, however denominated, but does not include professional services rendered by a member of a learned profession. (c) Nothing in this section shall apply to acts done by the publisher, owner, agent, or employee of a newspaper, periodical or radio or television station, or other advertising medium in the publication or dissemination of an advertisement, when the owner, agent or employee did not have knowledge of the false, misleading or deceptive character of the advertisement and when the newspaper, periodical or radio or television station, or other advertising medium did not have a direct financial interest in the sale or distribution of the advertised product or service. (d) Any party claiming to be exempt from the provisions of this section shall have the burden of proof with respect to such claim. (1969, c. 833; 1977, c. 747, ss. 1, 2.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the receiving party's responsibility for breaches caused by their employees or consultants. The Law Explanation suggests this could be an unfair trade practice under NCGS § 75-1.1 if it leads to competitive harm because it weakens the protection of confidential information. Scraped Law Snippet 2, which contains G.S. 75-1.1 § 75-1.1.(a), declares unlawful "unfair methods of competition in or affecting commerce, and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in or affecting commerce". By removing the explicit responsibility for employee/consultant breaches, the changed text creates a situation where inadequate protection of confidential information by a company could be deemed an unfair method of competition.

perturbation.original_text: The obligations of confidentiality under this Agreement shall not app ly to any information that: (a) was previously known to t he Parties free of any obligation to keep it confidential; (b) is or has become publicly known, through no wrongful act of either Party ; (c) was rightfully received from a third party who is under n o obligation of confidence to the receiving Party ; (d) wa s independently developed by the Parties without use of Confidential Information that has been disclosed pursuant to this 2 Agreement; or (e) was required to be disclosed in order to comply with applic able law or regulation (including without limitation for securities law purposes) or with any requirement imposed by any governmental or court order but only to the extent required.
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations of confidentiality under this Agreement shall not app ly to any information that: (a) was previously known to t he Parties free of any obligation to keep it confidential; (b) is or has become publicly known, through no wrongful act of either Party ; (c) was rightfully received from a third party who is under n o obligation of confidence to the receiving Party ; (d) wa s independently developed by the Parties without use of Confidential Information that has been disclosed pursuant to this 2 Agreement; or (e) was required to be disclosed in order to comply with applic able law or regulation or with any requirement imposed by any governmental or court order.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase "(including without limitation for securities law purposes) but only to the extent required." from exception (e), the agreement now has ambiguity about what information can be disclosed to comply with applicable law, removing clarity surrounding compliance with securities laws. This could be legally problematic, as it creates confusion regarding required disclosures under securities regulations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934, specifically provisions requiring disclosure of material information.
perturbation.law_citation: 15 U.S. Code § 78j - Manipulative and deceptive devices
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act and Rule 10b-5 prohibit manipulative and deceptive devices in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. Companies are required to disclose material information. Removing the clarification regarding disclosures for securities law purposes creates uncertainty, potentially leading to violations if parties are unclear on their disclosure obligations.

Manipulative and deceptive devices It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce or of the mails, or of any facility of any national securities exchange- (a)(1) To effect a short sale, or to use or employ any stop-loss order in connection with the purchase or sale, of any security other than a government security, in contravention of such rules and regulatio
perturbation.justification: The omission in the changed text creates a legal contradiction because it removes the specific reference to securities law compliance, potentially weakening the obligation to disclose information as required by securities regulations. The original text clarifies that disclosure is allowed 'for securities law purposes' and 'only to the extent required,' while the changed text lacks this clarity, creating ambiguity about disclosure obligations under securities law. The Law Explanation highlights that Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act and Rule 10b-5 prohibit manipulative and deceptive devices, requiring companies to disclose material information. By removing the phrase relating to securities law, the agreement now fails to clearly delineate the extent to which information can be disclosed for compliance with securities laws. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 15 USC 78j, addresses 'manipulative and deceptive devices' which relates to disclosure. The changed text undermines the original text's intention to clarify permissible disclosures, creating potential non-compliance issues.

perturbation.original_text: If a legal action between the Parties arises from this Agreement or the conduct of any Party with respect to any Confidential Information, the prevailing Party shall recover from the other Party its reasonable attorney fees and costs.
perturbation.changed_text: If a legal action between the Parties arises from this Agreement or the conduct of any Party with respect to any Confidential Information, the prevailing Party may recover from the other Party its attorney fees and costs.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall' to 'may' alters the mandatory nature of awarding attorney fees to the prevailing party. This omission weakens the protection for the disclosing party, especially if they need to enforce the NDA. This contradicts North Carolina's approach to protecting trade secrets, as it reduces the incentive to pursue legal action against breaches.
perturbation.contradicted_law: North Carolina Trade Secrets Protection Act, N.C. Gen. Stat. § 66-152 et seq.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not explicitly mandating attorney fees, the North Carolina Trade Secrets Protection Act aims to provide remedies for misappropriation of trade secrets. Making attorney fees discretionary weakens the act's intent to protect trade secrets by reducing the incentive for the victim of misappropriation to sue.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: ï»¿ G.S. 66-154 § 66-154. Remedies. (a) Except as provided herein, actual or threatened misappropriation of a trade secret may be preliminarily enjoined during the pendency of the action and shall be permanently enjoined upon judgment finding misappropriation for the period that the trade secret exists plus an additional period as the court may deem necessary under the circumstances to eliminate any inequitable or unjust advantage arising from the misappropriation. (1) If the court determines that it would be unreasonable to enjoin use after a judgment finding misappropriation, an injunction may condition such use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for any period the court may deem just. In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect the trade secret may be compelled by order of the court. (2) A person who in good faith derives knowledge of a trade secret from or through misappropriation or by mistake, or any other person subsequently acquiring the trade secret therefrom or thereby, shall be enjoined from disclosing the trade secret, but no damages shall be awarded against any person for any misappropriation prior to the time the person knows or has reason to know that it is a trade secret. If the person has substantially changed his position in good faith reliance upon the availability of the trade secret for future use, he shall not be enjoined from using the trade secret but may be required to pay a reasonable royalty as deemed just by the court. If the person has acquired inventory through such knowledge or use of a trade secret, he can dispose of the inventory without payment of royalty. If his use of the trade secret has no adverse economic effect upon the owner of the trade secret, the only available remedy shall be an injunction against disclosure. (b) In addition to the relief authorized by subsection (a), actual damages may be recovered, measured by the economic loss or the unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation of a trade secret, whichever is greater.
perturbation.justification: The change from "shall" to "may" significantly alters the obligation regarding attorney fees for the prevailing party. The original text mandates the recovery of attorney fees, while the changed text makes it discretionary. This weakens the protection for the disclosing party in an NDA. The Law Explanation suggests this change undermines the intent of trade secret protection by reducing the incentive to sue for misappropriation. Scraped Law Snippet 2, G.S. 66-154, outlines remedies for misappropriation, but it doesn't explicitly mandate attorney fees. However, the modification removes a key protection, creating a legal loophole, thus the contradiction exists as the omission weakens the legal protection.
